text: So this is a video of a minimally invasive esophagectomy. This was performed at William Osler Health System in Brampton, Ontario. Now I've divided the procedure out into ten steps. Four laparoscopic steps and six thoracic steps.

So this is a video of a minimally invasive esophagectomy. This was performed at William Osler Health System in Brampton, Ontario. Now I've divided the procedure out into ten steps. Four laparoscopic steps and six thoracic steps.

The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy to check for metastatic deposits before starting the minimally invasive Ira Lewis resection. The procedure begins with the division of the gastropathic ligament, and a liver retractor is prepared for use.

The surgeon begins the minimally invasive Ira Lewis resection by performing a diagnostic laparoscopy to check for metastatic deposits. The gastropathic ligament is then taken, and a liver retractor is positioned to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to mobilize the hiatus, navigating through challenging tissue planes that may be obliterated due to prior neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to mobilize the hiatus, navigating through challenging tissue planes that may be obliterated due to prior neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the stomach after completing the hiatal dissection, carefully avoiding the gastro-epiploic artery. Using the harmonic scalpel, the surgeon grabs omental vessels to create an opening, allowing entry into the lesser sac, and proceeds with dissection.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the stomach after completing the hiatal dissection, carefully avoiding the gastro-epiploic artery. Using the harmonic scalpel, they dissect away from the stomach, grasping omental vessels to create an opening into the lesser sac.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the short gastric vessels, staying well away from the greater curvature of the stomach to avoid the gastroepiploic artery. The dissection progresses closer to the greater curvature as the gastroepiploic artery diminishes, with careful attention to avoid bleeding from the short gastric vessels. The dissection is directed towards the pylorus.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the short gastric vessels, maintaining a safe distance from the greater curvature of the stomach to avoid the gastroepiploic artery. The dissection progresses closer to the greater curvature as the gastroepiploic artery is no longer a concern, while being mindful of the high-riding splenic flexure to prevent an inadvertent colotomy.

The surgeon maintains visibility of the gastroepiploic artery while continuing the dissection towards the pylorus. Retrogastric adhesions are carefully taken down, avoiding injury to the gastroduodenal artery. This reveals a clear view of the anterior surface of the pancreas.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes retrogastric adhesions, ensuring not to injure the gastroduodenal artery. Once the adhesions are cleared, the anterior surface of the pancreas becomes clearly visible.

The surgeon continues dissecting retrogastric adhesions, progressing towards the left gastric lymphovascular bundle. Omental vessels are taken to gain additional length on the gastric conduit. After mobilizing most of the stomach, attention is turned to the left gastric artery.

The surgeon continues dissecting retrogastric adhesions, progressing towards the left gastric lymphovascular bundle. Omental vessels are taken to gain additional length on the gastric conduit. The stomach is mostly mobilized, and attention is turned to the left gastric artery, with the peritoneal covering of the vessels cleared. The posterior aspect of the right crus is mobilized to access the left gastric artery.

The surgeon clears the peritoneal covering from the vessels and mobilizes the posterior aspect of the right crus to access the left gastric artery. A stapler with a vascular load is used to staple off the base of the left gastric artery and vein.

The surgeon uses a vascular stapler to transect the base of the left gastric artery and vein. After the first staple firing, the exposure of the distal esophagus is improved. The stapling of the left gastric bundle is completed with an additional firing.

After the initial stapling, the surgeon achieves improved exposure of the distal esophagus and completes the stapling of the left gastric bundle. The harmonic scalpel is used to finish removing adhesions at the hiatus level. The focus then shifts to creating the gastric conduit.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to finish dissecting adhesions at the hiatus. Attention is then turned to creating the gastric conduit, starting with a gray load stapler to manage the vascular fat along the lesser curve. The conduit is crafted to be approximately four centimeters wide, ensuring it is neither too wide nor too tight. The apex of the stomach is pulled into the left upper quadrant to prevent folding during the final stapling steps.

The surgeon uses a gray load stapler to create a four-centimeter wide gastric conduit along the lesser curve, managing the vascular fat to prevent bleeding. The apex of the stomach is pulled into the left upper quadrant to prevent folding during the final stapling. Occasionally, the right lower quadrant port is used to adjust the stomach position for easier stapling. A feeding tube is inserted as the next step.

The surgeon uses the right lower quadrant port to pull the stomach down, straightening the gastric conduit for easier stapling. A feeding tube is placed in the left lower quadrant, identified with a 22-gauge needle. A stay suture is secured from the abdominal wall to the anti-mesenteric side of the jejunum, approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz. The laparoscopic Barone feeding jejunostomy set is used, with a dilator and feeding tube inserted, and sixty cc's of saline pushed into the tube to facilitate distal advancement.

The surgeon identifies an area in the left lower quadrant and uses a 22-gauge needle to mark the spot. A stay suture is placed from the abdominal wall to the anti-mesenteric side of the jejunum, approximately thirty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz. The endo-stitch is used to secure the jejunum to the abdominal wall. The laparoscopic Barone feeding jejunostomy set is utilized, and the feeding tube is inserted with a dilator, followed by the infusion of sixty cc's of saline into the tube as it is advanced.

The surgeon places an anti-volvulus stitch approximately three to four centimeters distal to the feeding tube that is tacked to the abdominal wall. Following this, the surgeon aggressively dissects the mediastinal area, navigating unclear tissue planes due to the patient's prior neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy.

The surgeon places an anti-volvulus stitch approximately three to four centimeters distal to the feeding tube that is tacked to the abdominal wall. Following this, the surgeon aggressively dissects the mediastinal tissue, navigating through unclear planes due to the patient's prior neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy.

The surgeon enters the left pleural space, causing the diaphragm to appear floppy. The lung is visible, and if mediastinal fat is attached to the pleura, the pleura is taken. The conduit is sutured to the specimen in the anatomical position, ensuring the staple line faces the surgeon when pulled into the chest. The video transitions to the VATS portion of the procedure.

The surgeon enters the left pleural space, causing the diaphragm to appear floppy. The lung is visible, and the pleura is dissected if attached to the mediastinal fat. The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, and the gastric conduit is sutured to the specimen in an anatomical position. The VATS procedure begins with the dissection of the inferior pulmonary ligament, exposing the laparoscopic work and mobilizing the ligament up to the inferior pulmonary vein. A diaphragm stitch is placed for better exposure.

The surgeon begins by taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament, exposing previous laparoscopic work. The ligament is mobilized up to the inferior pulmonary vein. A diaphragm stitch is placed to enhance exposure and prevent obstruction of the camera view. Attention is then turned to the azygos vein, which is mobilized both inferiorly and superiorly before being transected using a vascular load.

The surgeon mobilizes the azygos vein inferiorly and superiorly before transecting it with a vascular load. The mediastinal pleura is then dissected using a harmonic scalpel, extending the dissection down to the level of the azygos vein and further to the level of the hiatus.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect the mediastinal pleura, extending the dissection down to the level of the azygos vein and further to the hiatus.

The surgeon begins mediastinal lymph node dissection and mobilization of the esophagus, working anteriorly to sweep subcranial nodes into the specimen. The area is vascular, requiring clipping of aeroesophageal vessels to prevent bleeding. The esophagus is swept off the pericardium, and nodes are incorporated into the specimen. Once half of the esophagus is mobilized, the gastric conduit is pulled into the chest for better mobility. The conduit is disconnected from the esophagus, and clips are used liberally during medial dissection. The thoracic duct is controlled with clips if visible. Dissection continues at the level of the azagus vein, improving exposure by pulling the esophagus up.

The surgeon divides the pleura and begins mediastinal lymph node dissection while mobilizing the esophagus. Working anteriorly, subcranial nodes are swept into the specimen side. The area is vascular, requiring clipping of aeroesophageal vessels to prevent bleeding. Lymph nodes are kept with the specimen for an en bloc lymphadenectomy, although some may be removed separately. The esophagus is swept off the pericardium, and once half is mobilized, the gastric conduit is pulled into the chest to facilitate further esophageal dissection.

The surgeon adjusts the staple line orientation while pulling up the esophagus, ensuring it faces correctly. The conduit is disconnected from the esophagus, and the dissection continues medially with liberal use of clips. The dissection is performed at the level of the azygos vein, with improved exposure due to the upward traction on the esophagus.

The surgeon clips and transects the thoracic duct, followed by transecting the esophagus with a blue load stapler inserted through the anterior ten millimeter port. The Orville stapler is used to create an anastomosis, with the anvil passed through the mouth using an orogastric tube. A small hole is made on the esophageal staple line, and the anvil is pulled through, detached from the tube using a Swedish DeBakey. A purse string is placed around the anvil to ensure snug closure, facilitating the creation of two complete donuts when firing the EEA stapler. The muscle retracts slightly, but the purse string effectively brings the esophageal wall together. A gastrotomy is created along the staple line, ensuring the gastric conduit is correctly positioned.

The surgeon clips and transects the thoracic duct, followed by mobilizing and transecting the esophagus using a blue load stapler from the anterior ten millimeter port. The Orville stapler is used to create an anastomosis, with the anvil passed through the mouth via an orogastric tube. A small hole is made on the esophageal staple line, and the anvil is pulled through, detached from the tube using a Swedish DeBakey. A purse string is placed around the anvil to secure the esophageal wall, ensuring two complete donuts when firing the EEA stapler. The muscle retraction is visible, and a gastrotomy is created along the staple line, maintaining the correct position of the gastric conduit.

The surgeon opens the stomach, preparing for potential spillage of gastric contents into the pleural space, and uses suction to manage it. The stapler is passed into the chest, and the gastric conduit is pulled into the stapler using the 'foot going into the sock' technique. The needle is deployed and mated with the anvil, assisted by the Swedish DeBakey holding the anvil's white part. The stapler is removed, and the completeness of the donuts is examined. An NG tube is then passed distally into the gastric conduit.

The surgeon opens the stomach, preparing suction to manage potential spillage of gastric contents into the pleural space. The stapler is passed into the chest, and the gastric conduit is pulled into the stapler using the 'foot going into the sock' technique. The needle is deployed and mated with the anvil, assisted by a Swedish DeBakey to hold the anvil in place. The stapler is removed, and the completeness of the donuts is examined. An NG tube is then passed distally into the gastric conduit.

The surgeon uses a thirty-degree camera to visualize the anastomosis. After placing the NG tube, the gastrotomy is closed with one or two firings of the blue load stapler. An omental flap is used to cover the anastomosis, which is then sutured to the prevertebral fascia.

Using a thirty-degree camera, the surgeon visualizes the anastomosis. After placing the NG tube, the gastrotomy is closed with one or two firings of the blue load stapler. An omental flap, if created, is used to cover the anastomosis and is sutured to the prevertebral fascia.
